rationale: The cited paper is discussed in the conceptual development, not just listed in background citations. The citing paper uses Khan, Zhu, and Kalra (2011) as direct empirical support that higher construal from temporal and spatial distance shifts trade-off processing and increases selection of higher-priced options (including attenuation of the compromise effect). Those results are used to justify the citing paper's predictions about how booking lead time and travel distance affect hotel quality-price choices. Although the citing study does not replicate the cited paper's experiments, the citation meaningfully informs its theoretical framing and hypothesis logic.
relation_description: The citing paper relies on Khan et al. (2011) as key prior evidence within construal-level theory to argue that greater psychological distance changes quality-price weighting in choice. It uses that foundation to motivate its own hypotheses in an online hotel booking and satisfaction context.
